questions.q: Which stage is primarily responsible for turning raw text into semantic units for search?
questions.explain: Indexing splits text into chunks and stores chunk embeddings in a vector store.
questions.hint: This is the stage before the system can do fast semantic lookup.
questions.explain_simple: Indexing is the step that converts text pieces into searchable representations and persists them.

questions.q: Which tool is commonly used as the vector database in a RAG pipeline?
questions.explain: ChromaDB stores vector embeddings and supports nearest-neighbor retrieval with distance/similarity scoring.
questions.hint: It is one of the most commonly used local-friendly vector stores.
questions.explain_simple: In this template, ChromaDB is used as a vector database that keeps chunk embeddings and finds the closest matches.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the function expand_query?
options: To split long answers into bullet points
To transform user intent into a richer retrieval query
questions.explain: Query expansion makes retrieval more context-friendly before vector search.
questions.hint: This step is a pre-processing step before retrieval.
questions.explain_simple: The query is rewritten into a broader form so the retrieval stage can find better-matching chunks.

questions.q: What is the main role of top_k in retrieval?
options: Number of tokens sent to the model
Number of chunks returned from vector search
questions.explain: top_k controls how many top-matching chunks are fetched from the vector store.
questions.hint: This number defines how many candidate chunks become context.
questions.explain_simple: A higher top_k adds more retrieved chunks; a lower one is more selective.

questions.q: Why is a similarity threshold used in ask()/generation flow?
options: To force the model to always answer
To protect against hallucinations when evidence is weak
questions.explain: If top score is below threshold, the system should return a safe refusal.
questions.hint: This is a confidence gate before generating the final response.
questions.explain_simple: The threshold prevents low-confidence answers by refusing to answer when evidence is weak.

questions.q: What is a typical safe refusal message used in RAG systems?
options: I am not sure, but maybe
Information is not available in the documents.
questions.explain: The system uses a fixed refusal text when context is insufficient.
questions.hint: The message must communicate missing evidence clearly.
questions.explain_simple: When retrieval evidence is weak, a safe response is better than fabricating an answer.

questions.q: How is similarity score calculated in this pipeline?
questions.explain: Chroma returns distance; a compatibility conversion is often done as score = 1 / (1 + distance).
questions.hint: A lower distance means higher similarity after conversion.
questions.explain_simple: Distance is turned into an intuitive score-like value so closer chunks get larger numbers.

questions.q: What is Recall@k measuring in a RAG evaluation?
options: Whether correct evidence appears in the top-k retrieved chunks
How many tokens the LLM used
questions.explain: Recall@k is a retrieval coverage metric over the top-k candidates.
questions.hint: Focus on retrieval coverage, not generation quality.
questions.explain_simple: Recall@k checks if the right evidence is present among the first k retrieved chunks.

options: How long each answer takes to generate
Rank quality of the first correct relevant chunk
questions.explain: MRR rewards a correct chunk being placed near the top of the ranking.
questions.hint: It values earlier correct positions more than later ones.
questions.explain_simple: If the first relevant chunk is at rank 1, MRR is high; if it is at rank 5, it is lower.

questions.q: Which strategy improved answer quality the most in this RAG experiment sequence?
questions.explain: The setup shows strongest final gains from hybrid retrieval plus reranking.
questions.hint: This combines broader retrieval with a second-pass quality filter.
questions.explain_simple: Expanding queries and then reranking results tends to improve final relevance of context.

questions.q: Why was re-rank output changed to selected chunk text rather than index numbers in version 2?
options: Because index numbers were often returned incorrectly by the model
questions.explain: Returning raw chunk text avoids index parsing errors and mismatch risks.
questions.hint: Model output reliability is important in downstream prompt assembly.
questions.explain_simple: Using the chunk body directly prevents failures caused by wrong or mismatched IDs.

questions.q: Which function is expected to use both retrieval and generation logic in one full user query?
questions.explain: ask() retrieves evidence, checks threshold, builds context, and prompts the LLM to answer.
questions.explain_simple: ask() is the entry point that ties retrieval and generation into one answer flow.

questions.q: What is the role of citations in RAG answers?
options: To force the model to ignore context
To provide source traceability in the answer
questions.explain: Citations like [filename, Chunk X] make claims auditable.
questions.hint: Think about tracing each claim back to source.
questions.explain_simple: Citations keep answers transparent by pointing to where each statement came from.

options: Overlap between consecutive chunks to preserve context continuity
questions.explain: Overlap keeps key phrases from splitting across boundaries.
questions.hint: This is a tuning parameter during splitting.
questions.explain_simple: A small overlap helps preserve meaning when important context spans a boundary.

questions.q: Which output is most useful during evaluation beyond correctness?
questions.explain: The pipeline logs latency and token usage to evaluate efficiency as well as accuracy.
questions.explain_simple: Efficiency metrics help you judge whether the system is practical in production.
